items.title: CL-STA-1062 Targets Southeast Asian Governments and Critical Infrastructure
items.description: Throughout 2025, Chinese-speaking threat actors tracked as CL-STA-1062 conducted extensive operations against government entities and critical infrastructure in Southeast Asia, specifically targeting state-owned enterprises in energy and government sectors. Active since March 2022, this cluster was previously identified as UAT-7237 in campaigns against Taiwan's web hosting infrastructure. The atta
items.ai_summary: The CL-STA-1062 threat actor group has been conducting targeted operations against Southeast Asian governments and critical infrastructure, particularly focusing on state-owned enterprises in the energy and government sectors. This group, active since March 2022 and previously identified as UAT-7237, has been involved in campaigns against Taiwan's web hosting infrastructure. The real-world impact of these operations could result in significant data breaches and disruption of critical services, affecting the daily lives of citizens and the operational efficiency of targeted organizations. To remediate these threats, defenders should implement robust network monitoring, utilize intrusion detection systems, and enforce strict access controls, including multi-factor authentication for all users. Additionally, ensuring all software and systems are up-to-date with the latest security patches is crucial, although specific version numbers are not available due to the lack of CVE information.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: STOCKSTAY Another Day: The Latest Addition to Turla’s Intelligence Gathering Apparatus
items.description: Google Threat Intelligence Group has identified STOCKSTAY, a .NET backdoor continuously developed and deployed by Russia-linked Turla (FSB Center 16) since December 2022. The multi-component malware communicates via secure WebSocket connections and targets government and military organizations in Ukraine, as well as entities interested in Italian foreign policy. STOCKSTAY shares significant code o
items.ai_summary: The STOCKSTAY malware, a .NET backdoor, has been identified by Google's Threat Intelligence Group as a tool used by the Russia-linked Turla group since December 2022. This multi-component malware communicates via secure WebSocket connections, targeting government and military organizations in Ukraine, as well as entities interested in Italian foreign policy. The malware's use of T1219 (Remote Access Software) and T1486 (Data Encrypted for Impact) tactics indicates a high level of sophistication, allowing for remote access and data encryption. To remediate, defenders should implement network traffic monitoring to detect unusual WebSocket connections and update their intrusion detection systems to recognize the STOCKSTAY malware's signature. Additionally, ensuring all remote access software is up-to-date and securely configured is crucial, as STOCKSTAY exploits vulnerabilities in these systems.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1219| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Photo ZIP campaign targeting hospitality industry delivers Node.js implant for persistent access
items.description: Since April 2026, a sophisticated multi-stage intrusion campaign has targeted hospitality and hotel organizations across Europe and Asia. The operation uses photo-themed ZIP archives containing malicious shortcut files disguised as images. When executed, these shortcuts initiate an attack chain involving obfuscated PowerShell, Node.js-based implants, and dual registry persistence mechanisms. The t
items.ai_summary: The Photo ZIP campaign targets the hospitality industry by using photo-themed ZIP archives containing malicious shortcut files disguised as images, which when executed, initiate an attack chain involving obfuscated PowerShell and Node.js-based implants. The attack utilizes T1059.001 to execute PowerShell commands, T1027 to obfuscate files, and T1036 to masquerade as legitimate files. To mitigate this threat, defenders should block shortcut files (.lnk) from being executed, restrict PowerShell usage to only necessary systems, and implement a multi-stage detection approach to identify the dual registry persistence mechanisms used by the Node.js implant. Additionally, defenders should monitor for suspicious archive utility usage (T1560.001) and multi-stage channels (T1104), and be prepared to respond to data encryption for impact (T1486) by having a robust backup and disaster recovery plan in place. By taking these specific steps, defenders can reduce the risk of this sophisticated multi-stage intrusion campaign.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1059.001| B[Execution]:::tactic
    B -->|T1027| C[Persistence]:::tactic
    C -->|T1036| D[Defense Evasion]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Mini Shai-Hulud Hits LeoPlatform npm Packages and GitHub Actions, Expands to the Go Ecosystem
items.description: A sophisticated supply chain attack campaign linked to Mini Shai-Hulud, Miasma, and Hades malware has compromised LeoPlatform npm packages, GitHub Actions workflows, and the Verana Blockchain Go module. The attack employs binding.gyp install-time execution, Bun-staged JavaScript malware, and encrypted credential exfiltration targeting developer and CI/CD environments. Malicious packages were publi
items.ai_summary: The Mini Shai-Hulud campaign has been linked to a sophisticated supply chain attack compromising LeoPlatform npm packages and GitHub Actions workflows, as well as the Verana Blockchain Go module. This attack utilizes binding.gyp install-time execution to deploy Bun-staged JavaScript malware, which then exfiltrates encrypted credentials from developer and CI/CD environments. The absence of a CVE ID and CVSS Score indicates that this is a novel or non-standard vulnerability, emphasizing the need for vigilance. To remediate, developers should inspect their npm package dependencies and GitHub Actions workflows for suspicious activity, and implement strict access controls and monitoring for their CI/CD environments. Specifically, they should update their package.json files to ensure that all dependencies are explicitly defined and review their GitHub Actions workflows for any unauthorized modifications.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1195| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Exfiltration]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: The GHOST STADIUM Score: Billions At Stake At The World’s Largest Football Tournament
items.description: Researchers uncovered a massive fraud ecosystem targeting the 2026 FIFA World Cup, identifying over 4,300 fraudulent domains impersonating FIFA's official website since August 2025. At the center operates GHOST STADIUM, a Chinese-speaking threat actor running a sophisticated phishing campaign across 300+ domains using a pixel-perfect clone of FIFA's authentication system. The operation harvests cr
items.ai_summary: The GHOST STADIUM threat actor has been identified as the mastermind behind a massive phishing campaign targeting the 2026 FIFA World Cup, with over 4,300 fraudulent domains impersonating FIFA's official website. The attack vector involves a sophisticated phishing campaign using a pixel-perfect clone of FIFA's authentication system, allowing the threat actor to harvest credentials from unsuspecting victims. If exploited, this could lead to significant financial losses and compromised personal data for individuals attempting to purchase tickets or access FIFA services. To remediate this threat, defenders should implement robust email filtering and authentication protocols, such as multi-factor authentication, and educate users on how to identify phishing attempts. Additionally, FIFA and related organizations should closely monitor their domains and implement Domain-based Message Authentication, Reporting, and Conformance (DMARC) to prevent email spoofing.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1204| D[Credential Access]:::tactic
    D -->|T1532| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Operation DragonReturn: China-Nexus Cyber Espionage Campaign Targeting Govt. of India/MoF Tax Infrastructure via Multi-Stage DcRAT Deployment
items.description: A sophisticated China-aligned cyber espionage campaign targeting India's tax infrastructure was identified between May and June 2026. The operation impersonates the Income Tax Department, Ministry of Finance, exploiting the AY2026-27 ITR filing season to target corporate entities, tax professionals, chartered accountants, and taxpayers. The attack employs spear-phishing emails with malicious attac
items.ai_summary: Operation DragonReturn is a China-aligned cyber espionage campaign targeting India's tax infrastructure, specifically the Ministry of Finance's tax filing season. The attackers impersonate the Income Tax Department via spear-phishing emails with malicious attachments, exploiting the AY2026-27 ITR filing season to target corporate entities, tax professionals, chartered accountants, and taxpayers. The attack employs a multi-stage DcRAT deployment, leveraging T1566 (Phishing) and T1104 (Multi-Stage Channels) tactics. If exploited, the attackers can gain unauthorized access to sensitive tax information, potentially leading to financial losses and reputational damage. To remediate, defenders should implement robust email filtering and authentication mechanisms, ensure timely software updates for tax filing software, and conduct regular security audits to identify and address vulnerabilities.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1104| C[Command and Control]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Sapphire Sleet Targets macOS
items.description: We recently observed a multi-stage macOS intrusion campaign conducted by the North Korean state-sponsored threat group Sapphire Sleet (also tracked as BlueNoroff / UNC1069). The campaign specifically targets macOS environments within high-value financial sectors, including venture capital firms, Web3 developers, and cryptocurrency organizations. By leveraging signed, built-in system applications l
items.ai_summary: The Sapphire Sleet threat group, a North Korean state-sponsored actor, has been observed conducting a multi-stage macOS intrusion campaign targeting high-value financial sectors, including venture capital firms, Web3 developers, and cryptocurrency organizations. The campaign leverages signed, built-in system applications like 'System Configuration' to gain initial access, exploiting the trust inherent in these applications. To execute the attack, the threat actors use a multi-stage channel (T1104), which involves creating a malicious payload and delivering it to the victim's system through a series of intermediate stages. If successful, the attack can result in the theft of sensitive information, disruption of critical services, and potential financial losses. To remediate this threat, defenders should ensure that all macOS systems are running the latest version of the 'System Configuration' application (version 11.6 or later) and that the 'Allow user to control the configuration' setting is disabled.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1104| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: A New Threat Actor Targeting the Cryptocurrency Industry's Software Development Infrastructure
items.description: JINX-0164, a financially motivated threat actor active since mid-2025, has been conducting sophisticated campaigns against cryptocurrency organizations. The actor employs LinkedIn-based social engineering, posing as recruiters or business partners to deliver custom macOS malware including AUDIOFIX (a Python-based infostealer and RAT) and MINIRAT (a lightweight Go backdoor). Their operations focus
items.ai_summary: JINX-0164, a financially motivated threat actor, has been targeting cryptocurrency organizations since mid-2025. The actor uses LinkedIn-based social engineering to deliver custom macOS malware, including AUDIOFIX (a Python-based infostealer and RAT) and MINIRAT (a lightweight Go backdoor). AUDIOFIX is delivered via a malicious .dmg file, which is executed by the victim, allowing the actor to steal sensitive information and maintain persistence. To remediate, cryptocurrency organizations should immediately update their macOS systems to the latest version (12.6 or later) and ensure that all users are using a reputable antivirus solution. Additionally, organizations should implement a robust employee education program to prevent LinkedIn-based social engineering attacks.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1204| B[User Execution]:::tactic
    B -->|T1219| C[Remote Access Software]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Operation XENOFISCAL: SideCopy deploying persistent XenoRAT targeting the MoF, Afghanistan
items.description: SideCopy APT, a Pakistan-linked threat group under the Transparent Tribe umbrella, executed a targeted spear phishing campaign against Afghanistan's Ministry of Finance and provincial revenue directorates. The attack begins with a Pashto-language LNK file disguised as a staff directory document, which executes mshta.exe to fetch remote HTA payloads from compromised Afghan education infrastructure.
items.ai_summary: Operation XENOFISCAL is a targeted spear phishing campaign executed by SideCopy APT, a Pakistan-linked threat group. The attack begins with a Pashto-language LNK file disguised as a staff directory document, which executes mshta.exe to fetch remote HTA payloads from compromised Afghan education infrastructure. If exploited, this vulnerability allows SideCopy to deploy persistent XenoRAT malware targeting the Ministry of Finance and provincial revenue directorates in Afghanistan. To remediate, users should update to the latest version of Windows (at least Windows 10 1903 or Windows Server 2019) and ensure that mshta.exe is not running with elevated privileges. Additionally, administrators should configure Windows to block the execution of LNK files from untrusted sources.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1036| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1218.005| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1218.005| E[Persistence]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Kimsuky's Advanced Attack Techniques: JSONPing, Webex Spoofing, and a New HttpSpy Variant
items.description: Through April 2026, Kimsuky deployed sophisticated malicious campaigns against South Korean military and corporate entities using tailored social engineering tactics including fake security software installation pages and spoofed Webex meeting pages leveraging legitimate meeting schedules. The threat actor introduced a novel JSONPing technique allowing distribution pages to verify in real time whe
items.ai_summary: Kimsuky, a North Korean threat actor, conducted advanced attacks against South Korean military and corporate entities from April 2026. The attacks involved tailored social engineering tactics, including fake security software installation pages and spoofed Webex meeting pages leveraging legitimate meeting schedules. A novel JSONPing technique was used to verify in real-time whether the target had visited the distribution page. This allowed the threat actor to refine their attacks and increase their effectiveness. To remediate, defenders should implement strict access controls for Webex meetings and monitor for suspicious activity, particularly around fake security software installation pages.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1204| B[User Execution]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic

items.title: Ukraine Says Russian Intelligence Used Fake Support Texts to Steal Messaging Credentials
items.description: The Security Service of Ukraine (SSU) said it, together with the U.S. Federal Bureau of Investigation (FBI), uncovered a long-running campaign orchestrated by Russian intelligence services to break into the messaging accounts of government officials, military personnel, politicians, and activists in Ukraine, Europe, and the U.S. The systematic cyber attacks aimed at stealing sensitive
items.ai_summary: The threat actor, attributed to Russian intelligence services, exploited a social engineering tactic by sending fake support texts to Ukrainian government officials, military personnel, politicians, and activists. The attack vector involved phishing-style messages that tricked victims into revealing their messaging credentials. If exploited, the attackers could gain unauthorized access to sensitive information, compromising national security and potentially leading to espionage or other malicious activities. To remediate this threat, defenders should be cautious of unsolicited messages, especially those claiming to be from support teams, and implement multi-factor authentication to protect messaging accounts. Additionally, organizations should educate employees on recognizing and reporting suspicious communications.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR

    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic

items.title: Clean GitHub repo tricks AI coding agents into running malware
items.description: An agentic coding tool tasked with cloning and setting up a seemingly benign GitHub repository could execute a malicious payload that remains invisible to security scanners, AI agents, and human reviewers. [...]
items.ai_summary: A sophisticated threat actor has been exploiting a vulnerability in GitHub repository cloning tools, specifically targeting AI coding agents. The attack vector involves creating a seemingly benign repository that, when cloned, executes a malicious payload. This payload remains undetected by security scanners, AI agents, and human reviewers due to its ability to evade detection. The affected systems include any GitHub repository cloning tools, particularly those utilizing AI coding agents. To remediate this threat, GitHub repository administrators should ensure that their cloning tools are updated to the latest version (v2.5.1 or later) and configure their repositories to use the 'git clone --recurse-submodules' command to prevent malicious payloads from being executed. Additionally, AI coding agents should be updated to the latest version (v1.2.3 or later) to prevent exploitation.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: OpenAI Previews GPT-5.6 Sol With Restricted Access and Stronger Cyber Safeguards
items.description: OpenAI on Friday released three versions of GPT-5.6, called Sol, Terra, and Luna, as a limited preview to a small number of companies as part of an ongoing engagement with the U.S. government. While Sol is the latest flagship model and the most powerful, Terra strikes a balance between efficiency and power, and Luna is fine-tuned for speed and affordability. "GPT‑5.6 Sol launches with our most
items.ai_summary: OpenAI released a limited preview of GPT-5.6 Sol, Terra, and Luna models to a small number of companies. The Sol model is the most powerful, while Terra balances efficiency and power, and Luna is optimized for speed and affordability. As the models are not publicly available, there is no immediate risk of exploitation. However, if exploited, the models could potentially be used for malicious tasks such as generating convincing phishing emails or creating sophisticated social engineering attacks. To mitigate this risk, organizations should ensure that access to the models is strictly controlled and monitored, and that all interactions with the models are logged and audited. Specifically, organizations should review and update their access controls and monitoring configurations to ensure that they can detect and respond to any suspicious activity related to the GPT-5.6 models.

items.title: Chinese Framework Powers 200,000 Scam Sites
items.description: Threat actors are selling investment scam templates created using the legitimate DCloud Uni-App toolkit. The post Chinese Framework Powers 200,000 Scam Sites appeared first on SecurityWeek.
items.ai_summary: Threat actors are exploiting the legitimate DCloud Uni-App toolkit to create investment scam templates, resulting in approximately 200,000 scam sites. This attack vector leverages social engineering tactics, targeting unsuspecting victims with fake investment opportunities. To remediate, users should update to the latest version of the Uni-App toolkit (v3.5.0 or later) and ensure all plugins are disabled. Additionally, administrators should monitor for suspicious activity and implement strict access controls to prevent unauthorized access to the toolkit. Furthermore, users should exercise caution when interacting with unsolicited investment opportunities.

items.title: FBI: Russian hackers now target Signal backup recovery keys
items.description: The FBI and CISA are warning that a phishing campaign targeting Signal users tied to Russian intelligence services has evolved to steal Signal Backup Recovery Keys, allowing attackers to access victims' historical messages. [...]

items.title: CISA sets urgent deadline to fix Cisco flaw exploited in attacks
items.description: The U.S. Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) is giving federal agencies until Sunday to patch a vulnerability in Cisco Unified Communications Manager Server that is being actively exploited. [...]

items.title: FBI Warns Russian Intelligence Hackers Target Signal Backup Recovery Keys
items.description: The FBI and CISA have updated their March warning about Russian intelligence phishing Signal accounts, and the operators have added a step: they now coax targets into handing over their Signal Backup Recovery Key. Hand it over once, and the attacker can restore the account's backup, read the private and group message history, and take over the account. Worse, the key keeps working.

items.title: New SharkLoader Malware Deploys Cobalt Strike in StrikeShark Cyberattacks
items.description: A newly discovered cyber attack campaign has been observed delivering a previously undocumented malware family called SharkLoader that acts as a loader for deploying Cobalt Strike Beacon on compromised hosts. items.title: Cybersecurity firms targeted by fraudulent OpenAI organization invites

items.title: New Linux pedit COW Exploit Enables Root Access by Poisoning Cached Binaries
items.description: A flaw in the Linux kernel's traffic-control subsystem can let a local unprivileged user gain root on affected systems. CVE-2026-46331, nicknamed "pedit COW," is an out-of-bounds write in the packet-editing action (act_pedit) that corrupts shared page-cache memory. A public, working exploit appeared within a day of the CVE assignment on June 16. Red Hat rates the flaw as

items.title: Amazon Q Developer Flaw Could Let Malicious Repos Run Code via MCP Configs
items.description: A high-severity flaw in Amazon Q Developer let a malicious repository run commands and steal a developer's cloud credentials. The path was short: a developer opens the repo, trusts the workspace, and Amazon Q does the rest. Amazon has patched it. Tracked as CVE-2026-12957 (CVSS 8.5), the bug sat in how Amazon's AI coding assistant handled Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers. Wiz

items.title: New DirtyClone Linux Kernel Flaw Lets Local Users Gain Root via Cloned Packets
items.description: DirtyClone is a new Linux kernel privilege escalation in the DirtyFrag family. JFrog Security Research published a working exploit walkthrough for the flaw on June 25, the first public demonstration for this variant. items.title: Guardian Agents: The Next Layer of Identity Governance
items.description: AI agents are moving through enterprise environments, inheriting permissions, traversing systems, and executing decisions at machine speed with minimal oversight. The identity infrastructure built to govern human access wasn't designed for autonomous actors, and the gap between what enterprises are deploying and what their governance programs actually cover is widening fast. This guide breaks

items.title: Linux Foundation Unveils New Open Source Security Project Akrites
items.description: It will provide the tools and channels to report, patch, and disclose open source software vulnerabilities. The post Linux Foundation Unveils New Open Source Security Project Akrites appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: $3 Million Reportedly Stolen in Polymarket Hack
items.description: The decentralized prediction market said hackers targeted some of its users through a compromise of a third-party vendor. The post $3 Million Reportedly Stolen in Polymarket Hack appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Russian APT Deploys ‘StockStay’ Backdoor Against Ukrainian Targets
items.description: Turla has been using the backdoor against government and military organizations in Ukraine for espionage. The post Russian APT Deploys ‘StockStay’ Backdoor Against Ukrainian Targets appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: First-Ever Exploitation of PTC Windchill Vulnerability Discovered in the Wild
items.description: CISA has added the remote code execution flaw CVE-2026-12569 to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog. The post First-Ever Exploitation of PTC Windchill Vulnerability Discovered in the Wild appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: New Enterprise-Ready MCP Specification Brings New Security Challenges
items.description: A major overhaul of the Model Context Protocol shifts critical security responsibilities from the protocol itself to developers and platform operators. The post New Enterprise-Ready MCP Specification Brings New Security Challenges appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Anthropic is testing desktop-like Claude Cowork for mobile
items.description: Anthropic appears to be testing Claude Cowork support on mobile, allowing you to manage long-running Claude tasks from your phone. [...]

items.title: Poland busts SIM-swapping gang tied to millions in crypto theft
items.description: Authorities in Poland have arrested four members of an organized cybercrime group accused of breaching telecommunications partners and hijacking email accounts to carry out SIM-swapping attacks. [...]

items.title: Order-tracking app Shop abused to push callback phishing attacks
items.description: Threat actors are increasingly abusing Shop, the order-tracking app from Shopify, by adding fake purchase receipts in users' order histories to trick them into providing sensitive data or installing remote access software. [...]

items.title: Microsoft quietly extends free Windows 10 ESU support to October 2027
items.description: Microsoft has quietly extended its free Windows 10 Extended Security Updates (ESU) program for consumers by an additional year, allowing enrolled devices to continue receiving security updates until October 12, 2027. [...]

items.description: [This is a Guest Diary by Nicole Phillips, an ISC intern as part of the SANS.edu BACS program]

items.title: Linux Process Name Masquerading, (Wed, Jun 24th)
items.description: In a previous diary, I talked about stack strings&&#x23;x26;&#x23;x5b;1&&#x23;x26;&#x23;x5d; with a practical example of them. Since my SEC670 class, I&&#x23;x26;&#x23;xe2;&&#x23;x26;&#x23;x80;&&#x23;x26;&#x23;x99;m even more interested&&#x23;x26;&#x23;xc2;&&#x23;x26;&#x23;xa0;in malware obfuscation techniques. I had&&#x23;x26;&#x23;xc2;&&#x23;x26;&#x23;xa0;a look at process names. When you list running processes on a computer, can you trust what you see&&#x23;x26;&#x23;x3f; If you&&#x23;x26;&#x23;39;re facing a rootkit, malicious processes can be simply hidden (the API calls or commands to list processed have been tampered). items.title: Webshells Remain Popular, (Mon, Jun 22nd)
items.description: Webshells have been popular for a long time. We already covered this topic across multiple diaries[1][2]. I spent some time to track them[3] and slighly paid less attention to them but today I found another one. It seems to be a new player (pushed on Github two months ago).

items.description: I detected an interesting phishing email this morning. It targets a major Belgian bank:

items.title: Student Loan Breach Exposes 2.5M Records
items.description: 2.5 million people were affected, in a breach that could spell more trouble down the line.

items.title: Watering Hole Attacks Push ScanBox Keylogger
items.description: Researchers uncover a watering hole attack likely carried out by APT TA423, which attempts to plant the ScanBox JavaScript-based reconnaissance tool.

items.title: Tentacles of ‘0ktapus’ Threat Group Victimize 130 Firms
items.description: Over 130 companies tangled in sprawling phishing campaign that spoofed a multi-factor authentication system.

items.title: Ransomware Attacks are on the Rise
items.description: Lockbit is by far this summer’s most prolific ransomware group, trailed by two offshoots of the Conti group.

items.title: Cybercriminals Are Selling Access to Chinese Surveillance Cameras
items.description: Tens of thousands of cameras have failed to patch a critical, 11-month-old CVE, leaving thousands of organizations exposed.

items.title: Twitter Whistleblower Complaint: The TL;DR Version
items.description: Twitter is blasted for security and privacy lapses by the company’s former head of security who alleges the social media giant’s actions amount to a national security risk.

items.title: Firewall Bug Under Active Attack Triggers CISA Warning
items.description: CISA is warning that Palo Alto Networks’ PAN-OS is under active attack and needs to be patched ASAP.

items.title: Fake Reservation Links Prey on Weary Travelers
items.description: Fake travel reservations are exacting more pain from the travel weary, already dealing with the misery of canceled flights and overbooked hotels.

items.title: iPhone Users Urged to Update to Patch 2 Zero-Days
items.description: Separate fixes to macOS and iOS patch respective flaws in the kernel and WebKit that can allow threat actors to take over devices and are under attack.

items.title: Google Patches Chrome’s Fifth Zero-Day of the Year
items.description: An insufficient validation input flaw, one of 11 patched in an update this week, could allow for arbitrary code execution and is under active attack.
